expectations.text: Output contains a task overview or summary table listing all tasks
expectations.evidence: Lines 7-16 contain a '## Task Overview' section with a markdown table listing all 6 tasks, including columns for task number, name, dependencies, and parallelism.

expectations.text: Every task section includes a Description subsection with narrative context (not code)
expectations.evidence: All 6 tasks (Tasks 1-6) include a '### Description' subsection with multiple paragraphs of narrative prose explaining context, rationale, and technical decisions. No code appears in any description. For example, Task 1's description explains the motivation ('reduce infrastructure dependency on Redis'), the token architecture (access vs refresh), and the reasoning behind RS256 over HS256.

expectations.text: Every task section includes acceptance criteria using Given/When/Then scenarios
expectations.evidence: All 6 tasks have a '### Acceptance Criteria' subsection with multiple scenarios using explicit Given/When/Then format. Task 1 has 5 scenarios, Task 2 has 6, Task 3 has 6, Task 4 has 6, Task 5 has 6, and Task 6 has 5 — each consistently using the Given/When/Then pattern.

expectations.text: Every task section includes an Out of Scope subsection
expectations.evidence: All 6 tasks include a '### Out of Scope' subsection with bulleted items. For example, Task 1 excludes 'Modifying any existing session-based auth flow', 'API endpoint creation (that's Task 3)', 'Any frontend or mobile changes', and 'Redis session store modifications'.

expectations.text: No task punts context to 'see the spec' or 'refer to the spec' — all context is inline
expectations.evidence: Searched the full document for patterns like 'see the spec', 'refer to the spec', 'consult the spec' — no matches found. Each task contains all necessary context inline. Cross-references between tasks (e.g., 'using the API endpoints from Task 3') describe what's needed rather than deferring to external documents.

expectations.text: Tasks collectively cover API changes, frontend changes, mobile changes, and backward compatibility
expectations.evidence: API changes: Tasks 1 (JWT token infrastructure), 2 (dual-auth middleware), 3 (auth API endpoints), 6 (deprecation tooling). Frontend changes: Task 4 (React frontend JWT integration). Mobile changes: Task 5 (React Native mobile app JWT integration). Backward compatibility: Task 2 explicitly builds dual-auth middleware that accepts both sessions and JWT, with a scenario 'Existing session-based tests unchanged'; Task 6 builds gradual rollout tooling with feature flags.

expectations.text: Document uses Goal / Task Overview table / numbered Task sections pattern
expectations.evidence: Line 4: '**Goal:** Migrate authentication from session-based (Redis-backed) to JWT across the Express.js API, React frontend, and React Native mobile app while maintaining backward compatibility for a gradual rollout.' Lines 7-16: '## Task Overview' with a table. Lines 19-386: Six numbered sections '## Task 1' through '## Task 6'.

expectations.text: Output does not contain fenced code blocks, exact line number references (e.g., 'line 42'), or before/after implementation diffs
expectations.evidence: Searched for fenced code blocks (```), 'line N' references, and diff/before-after patterns — none found. The document is entirely prose, tables, and structured markdown without any code blocks or implementation-level references.

claims.claim: There are 6 tasks in the migration plan
claims.evidence: Counted 6 task sections (Task 1 through Task 6) in the document, matching the 6 rows in the Task Overview table.

claims.claim: Task dependency chain forms a valid DAG (1 -> 2 -> 3 -> [4, 5] -> 6)
claims.evidence: Table shows: Task 1 has no dependencies, Task 2 depends on 1, Task 3 depends on 2, Tasks 4 and 5 depend on 3 (and are marked parallel), Task 6 depends on 4 and 5. This is a valid directed acyclic graph.

claims.claim: Tasks are self-contained — each has enough context to be worked independently
claims.evidence: Each task includes full narrative context about why the work is needed, what technical decisions were made and why, and explicit acceptance criteria. Cross-references to other tasks describe the interface rather than deferring details.

claims.claim: Technical decisions include rationale, not just assertions
claims.evidence: For example, Task 1 explains RS256 choice: 'so that other services can verify tokens using only the public key without needing access to the signing secret.' Task 4 explains in-memory token storage: 'This limits XSS exposure.' Decisions consistently include reasoning.

suggestions.assertion: Every task section includes acceptance criteria using Given/When/Then scenarios
suggestions.reason: This checks format but not substance. A task could have Given/When/Then scenarios that are trivially vague (e.g., 'Given the system, When it works, Then it works') and still pass. Consider adding an expectation that scenarios reference specific HTTP status codes, token types, or error conditions — which this output does well but the assertion doesn't require.

No assertion checks whether the task dependencies form a valid, logical ordering. The overview table could list nonsensical dependencies (e.g., Task 1 depends on Task 6) and all 8 expectations would still pass. Consider adding: 'The Task Overview table shows a logical dependency chain where infrastructure tasks precede integration tasks.'

No assertion checks whether the References subsection exists in each task. This output includes References in all 6 tasks, which adds useful context about what code areas to look at. If References were dropped, it would reduce quality but no assertion would catch it.

eval_feedback.overall: The assertions cover the structural requirements well and all pass genuinely — this is a high-quality output. The main gap is that assertions check format/presence but not depth or correctness of content. The Given/When/Then scenarios in this output are substantive and specific, but the assertions would also pass for shallow ones.
